description: Ship `task-mgr add` CLI (JSON input + auto-priority + PRD JSON sync), `<task-status>TASK-ID:status</task-status>` bracket tag detection, iteration-prompt hardening, and an Edit/Write permission guard on `tasks/*.json`. Together these stop the loop agent from burning context rewriting the PRD JSON on every status flip or dynamic task insert.

criteria: Rust: No warnings from cargo check or cargo clippy --lib
Rust: All cargo test --lib cases pass
No breaking changes to existing CLI subcommand surfaces unless explicitly called out
Test + build commands use the single-command `tee | tail && grep` pattern (CLAUDE.md section 3 — needed because split commands hit the sandbox approval flow twice)
All match arms on `Commands` enum in src/cli/tests.rs updated when adding a new variant — otherwise CLI-test compilation fails (learning [1069])

priorityPhilosophy.description: The hierarchy of what matters most
hierarchy: 1. PLAN — Approach before code. Anticipate edge cases. Consider 2-3 approaches, pick the best
2. FUNCTIONING CODE — Pragmatic, reliable code, wired in according to the plan
3. CORRECTNESS — Self-critique after code. Compiles, type-checks, all tests pass
4. CODE QUALITY — Quality dimensions explicit. Clean code, good patterns, no warnings
principles: Quality dimensions explicit — read qualityDimensions on every task; know what 'good' looks like before coding
Approach before code — consider 2-3 approaches with tradeoffs, pick the best, then implement
Each task delivers working code + tests together
Self-critique after code — review your own implementation for correctness, style, and performance before moving on
Test boundaries and exceptions - edge cases are where bugs hide
Handle Option/Result explicitly; avoid unwrap() in production code
Reuse existing helpers — do NOT reinvent INSERT SQL, tag parsing, or status transition logic

prohibitedOutcomes: Tests that only assert 'no crash' or check type without verifying content
Tests that mirror implementation internals (break when refactoring)
Abstractions with only one concrete use
Error messages that don't identify what went wrong
Catch-all error handlers that swallow context
New INSERT SQL for tasks/task_files/task_relationships — reuse the helpers in src/commands/init/import.rs
New status-transition SQL — dispatch `<task-status>` through existing `complete`/`fail`/`skip`/`irrelevant`/`reset_tasks` command handlers
Rewriting the existing shipped commit 4eda061 (prompt-too-long + effort ladder) — it is already on main

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: `cargo build` succeeds on the feature branch after main.rs is wired
Positive: `cargo test --lib commands::add` runs at least 8 tests and all pass
Positive: `cargo clippy --lib -- -D warnings` clean
Positive: `cargo fmt --check` clean
Positive: `task-mgr add --help` (via `cargo run --bin task-mgr -- add --help`) prints the EXAMPLES and PRIORITY help blocks from the clap `after_help` attribute on the `Add` variant
Positive: git log shows exactly one new commit on the feature branch with a conventional-commits `feat:` prefix
Negative: No new INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE SQL added in this task — only reuse existing helpers from `src/commands/init/import.rs`
Negative: No changes to commit 4eda061 (prompt-too-long + effort ladder) — check with `git log main..HEAD`
Negative: No new files created besides the commit itself — this task only finalizes what's already in the working tree
Unit test (existing): `commands::add::tests::test_add_writes_task_into_database` passes
Unit test (existing): `commands::add::tests::test_auto_priority_one_less_than_top` passes — seeds one task at priority 10 and verifies new task gets priority 9

Known-bad: if main.rs routing is copied from another arm but stdin handling is skipped, `task-mgr add --stdin` would hang on stdin OR error unclearly. Verify manually: `echo '{...}' | cargo run -- add --stdin` must round-trip through the handler (failure case visible in integration tests in FEAT-002)

Failure mode: if `src/cli/tests.rs` has pattern-match code that doesn't compile because of the new variant, add the missing arm following the style of the `Commands::Reset` arm nearby — do not silence with `_ => {}` (that hides future regressions)
userStories.description:
The working tree already contains partial scaffolding for `task-mgr add`:
  - NEW: `src/commands/add.rs` (handler, `AddTaskInput`, `resolve_priority`, `locate_prd_json`, `append_task_to_prd_json`, `atomic_write`, unit tests)
  - MODIFIED: `src/commands/init/parse.rs` — `#[derive(Serialize)]` + `skip_serializing_if` on `PrdUserStory`
  - MODIFIED: `src/commands/mod.rs` — `pub mod add;` + re-exports
  - MODIFIED: `src/cli/commands.rs` — `Commands::Add { json, stdin, priority }` variant with EXAMPLES/PRIORITY help block
  - MODIFIED: `src/handlers.rs` — `impl_text_formattable!(AddResult, format_add_text)` at the top of the impl list

Your job: verify this scaffolding compiles, wire the `Commands::Add` match arm in `src/main.rs` (this is NOT yet done), update any broken exhaustive matches in `src/cli/tests.rs`, run the tests, and make the initial feature commit. This task's deliverable is a commit; it does NOT add new PRD-JSON-sync behaviour beyond what's already in `add.rs`.

DO: Read every modified file listed above BEFORE making changes. Add the main.rs routing arm next to `Commands::Reset` (around line 454). Run `cargo build`, `cargo test --lib commands::add`, `cargo clippy --lib -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`. If any exhaustive match over `Commands` in `src/cli/tests.rs` fails to compile because of the new variant, add the missing arm (learning [1069]).
DO NOT: Refactor the scaffolding. Do not change the tag name `<task-status>` or the auto-priority formula. Do not touch commits earlier than HEAD.

Edge cases:
- `src/cli/tests.rs` has multiple exhaustive matches on `Commands` (learning [1069]) — audit all of them; adding one missing arm is not enough if several exist.
- `src/handlers.rs` needs `pub use` of `format_add_text` for the macro invocation to resolve — verify already-done by reading the file.
- If `cargo build` fails with 'unused import' for `commands::add::*` after wiring main.rs, the re-exports in `src/commands/mod.rs` are already there — do not add a second `use` line in main.rs.

Known-bad to guard against:
- A stale or partial re-run that re-creates `src/commands/add.rs` from scratch — the file is already rich and tested; READ IT FIRST, do not overwrite.
- Forgetting that `src/cli/tests.rs` needs ALL exhaustive match arms updated, not just the one you encounter first (learning [1069]).
edgeCases: User passes neither --json nor --stdin → main.rs returns invalid_state error with a clear actionable message
User passes --json and --stdin together → clap rejects at parse time (conflicts_with) with its own diagnostic; no custom handling needed
stdin is empty → serde_json::from_str fails with a clear parse error; handler already surfaces it as invalid_state
Task id contains a single segment (e.g. 'FEAT-001') → task_prefix() returns None, auto-priority falls through to prefix=None branch of select_next_task (this is tested in add.rs)

failureModes.cause: Missing match arm in src/cli/tests.rs after adding Commands::Add variant
failureModes.expectedBehavior: cargo test fails with a clear `non-exhaustive patterns` error; fix is to add the arm using the Reset-arm shape

failureModes.cause: impl_text_formattable! macro invocation references a function that doesn't exist in the re-exports
failureModes.expectedBehavior: cargo build fails with `cannot find function format_add_text`; fix is to verify `pub use add::{..., format_text as format_add_text, ...}` in src/commands/mod.rs

userStories.notes:
Files to read BEFORE editing: src/commands/add.rs (full), src/cli/commands.rs:494 (the `Reset` variant — the Add variant is right above it), src/main.rs:454 (the Reset arm — your Add arm goes nearby), src/handlers.rs:74 (where impl_text_formattable! list starts), src/cli/tests.rs (grep for `Commands::Reset` to find exhaustive matches needing updates).

Key reuse: `commands::add::add()` public entry is already defined in add.rs; you only need to call it from main.rs.

Learnings:
- [1069]: Adding a clap enum variant breaks ALL exhaustive matches in src/cli/tests.rs — audit every one, don't patch just the first.
- [1479]: `impl_text_formattable!(AddResult, commands::format_add_text)` is already added in handlers.rs; verify it's still there after your edits.
- [1480]: Flat match dispatch in main.rs is idiomatic — copy the `Reset` arm shape, don't over-abstract.
- [1430]: Run tests via `cargo test --lib commands::add 2>&1 | tee /tmp/add-test.txt | tail -5 && grep FAILED /tmp/add-test.txt | head -10` — a single command (sandbox constraint).

correctness: Match arm in main.rs must accept either --json or --stdin and error clearly when both or neither are supplied (the clap `conflicts_with` handles the 'both' case at parse time; the 'neither' case needs a runtime check)
stdin read must use `std::io::Read::read_to_string` on `io::stdin()` — do NOT spawn a subshell, do NOT re-open /dev/stdin
All `cargo test --lib commands::add` tests pass without modification
performance: No redundant DB connections — a single `open_connection` + `LockGuard` per invocation (already in add.rs)
style: Match arm in main.rs mirrors the style of adjacent arms (use `&cli.dir`, call `output_result(&result, cli.format)`)
Use `crate::TaskMgrError::invalid_state` for the 'neither --json nor --stdin' error — follow the signature of existing invalid_state calls

userStories.title: Verify and commit the `task-mgr add` WIP scaffolding

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: new integration test file `tests/add_integration.rs` exists with at least 3 tests
Positive: `cargo test --test add_integration` runs and all tests pass
Positive: test_add_roundtrips_task_into_prd_json — assert DB state AND JSON file state after `add()`
Positive: test_auto_priority_derives_from_top — seeds priorities [50, 100], asserts new task gets 49
Positive: test_no_tmp_file_left_after_successful_add — verify no `.task-mgr-add.tmp` remains in the dir
Positive: test uses `tempfile::TempDir` and `task-mgr init --from-json` (via `commands::init::init()`) — no hardcoded paths
Negative: No modification to src/commands/add.rs beyond bug fixes surfaced by this test — if a test fails, fix the production code, don't weaken the assertion
Negative: No dependence on git state or the loop engine — this is a pure CLI-level integration test
Known-bad: a test that creates the DB via raw SQL instead of `task-mgr init` would pass even if prd_files is never populated, letting the PRD-sync path silently skip. Test MUST go through `init()`.
Edge-case test: PRD with `userStories: []` (empty array) — add() should succeed and produce an array with 1 element
Failure mode: if PRD JSON file is deleted after `init` but before `add`, `add()` logs a warning and the DB still gets the row (document this in the test assertion)
userStories.description:
The unit tests in `src/commands/add.rs` cover each helper in isolation, but there's no test that runs `task-mgr add` against a real initialized PRD and verifies BOTH the DB row AND the PRD JSON file on disk pick up the new task. Write an integration test that:
  1. Creates a tmp directory with a minimal PRD JSON (2 seed tasks)
  2. Runs `task-mgr init --from-json <prd>` to populate the DB + prd_files table
  3. Calls `task-mgr add` with a new task JSON (via `commands::add::add()` programmatically — don't spawn subprocesses, that's slow and fragile)
  4. Asserts: DB row exists with correct priority (auto-computed as top_priority - 1), `task_files` + `task_relationships` rows correct, and the PRD JSON file now has 3 entries in userStories with the new task appended and byte-for-byte unchanged fields on the original 2

DO: Put the test in `tests/add_integration.rs` (new file) so it compiles as a separate integration binary and has access to the public API. Use `tempfile::TempDir`.
DO NOT: Rely on the local `.task-mgr/` directory or any global state — everything must be tmp-dir scoped.

Edge cases to cover:
- Priority auto-compute correctness: seed PRD has 2 tasks at priorities 50 and 100 → new task priority must be 49 (one less than top), NOT 99 (one less than the OTHER one)
- `userStories` key ordering — serde_json preserves insertion order for the array, so the new task is LAST
- Original task JSON fields unchanged (read the seed PRD, add, re-read, diff the first 2 tasks' JSON Values — should be equal)
- Atomic write: after a successful add, there should be NO leftover `.add-cli-hardening.json.task-mgr-add.tmp` file in the tasks dir

Known-bad to guard against:
- A naive test that just checks the DB ignores the file half (half the feature). Test must assert BOTH halves.
- Testing with `serde_json::to_string` then a substring match on the new task ID: fragile; use `serde_json::from_str` and navigate the Value tree.
edgeCases: PRD file deleted between init and add → add logs warning, DB row still inserted
PRD file read-only → append_task_to_prd_json returns a clear error; DB row was inserted (non-atomic, documented)
Seed PRD has `userStories: []` → after add, array has 1 element
Seed PRD has `userStories` key missing entirely → after add, key is created with 1 element

failureModes.cause: Test uses raw SQL to seed tasks but doesn't populate prd_files — add() skips JSON sync path, test passes, real production would fail
failureModes.expectedBehavior: Test must go through `init()`; add-with-no-prd-file is a SEPARATE test case

userStories.notes:
Files to read: src/commands/add.rs (full — already has unit tests as reference for shape), src/commands/init/mod.rs (for the `init()` entry point), tests/cli_tests.rs (for integration-test idioms — how it uses TempDir, how it invokes commands programmatically).

Key types: `commands::init::init()` returns `InitResult`; `commands::add::add(db_dir, json_str, priority_override)` returns `AddResult`. Both take `&Path`. The `db_dir` is `<tmp>/.task-mgr`.

Learning [1069]: TempDir pattern — see existing tests like `tests/cli_tests.rs` for how to set up a tmp DB + PRD.

PRD JSON shape reference (from .task-mgr/tasks/batch-chain-worktrees.json): camelCase keys, `userStories` is the tasks array, `taskPrefix` optional.

correctness: Test must verify BOTH the DB row AND the PRD JSON entry — either alone is insufficient
Test must run against a real initialized DB (via `init()`), not a raw-SQL synthetic one
Test must clean up tmp dir on both success and failure (TempDir's Drop handles this)
performance: Test runtime <200ms — no network, no shell subprocesses
style: Use helper functions like `setup_prd_with_tasks(dir, seed_tasks)` to keep individual tests focused
Each test makes ONE logical assertion — DB-state test separate from JSON-state test separate from priority-derivation test

userStories.title: End-to-end integration test for `task-mgr add` — DB + PRD JSON round-trip

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: `extract_status_updates("<task-status>FEAT-001:done</task-status>")` returns `[TaskStatusUpdate { task_id: "FEAT-001", status: Done }]`
Positive: multiple tags in one output all parsed in order (test with 3 tags)
Positive: case-insensitive status parsing — `:DONE`, `:Done`, `:done` all map to `TaskStatusChange::Done`
Positive: engine dispatch calls `commands::complete::complete` for Done, `fail` for Failed, etc.
Positive: engine logs a warning and CONTINUES when a tag fails to dispatch (e.g., task not in_progress for Done)
Negative: No new SQL added — grep-verify that all INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on `tasks` in the dispatcher go through existing command handlers
Negative: `<task-status>` is NOT a variant of `IterationOutcome` — verify by reading the enum
Negative: Status-tag extraction does NOT alter the outcome returned by `analyze_output` — verify with an explicit test
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_single
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_multiple
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_case_insensitive_status
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_malformed_skipped
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_unknown_status_skipped
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_empty_id_skipped
Unit test: detection::tests::test_extract_status_updates_whitespace_trimmed

Unit test: detection::tests::test_status_tag_does_not_change_iteration_outcome — output contains both `<task-status>` and `<promise>COMPLETE</promise>` — outcome is Completed, AND status-update list is non-empty
Engine test: engine::tests::test_apply_status_update_marks_task_done_after_claim — seeds task in `in_progress`, runs dispatcher, asserts DB status transitions to `done`

Engine test: engine::tests::test_apply_status_update_logs_warning_when_task_not_claimed — seeds task in `todo` (not claimed), runs dispatcher, asserts task is still `todo` AND a warning was logged (learning [1475]: state machine violation should not silently succeed)
Engine test: engine::tests::test_apply_status_update_done_flips_prd_json_passes — after a successful Done dispatch, the PRD JSON entry's `passes` flips to true; other tasks in the array unchanged
Engine test: engine::tests::test_apply_status_update_json_sync_failure_does_not_rollback_db — if PRD JSON is missing/read-only, warning is logged but DB transition stands (mirrors add.rs behaviour)
Engine test: engine::tests::test_apply_status_update_task_missing_from_prd_json — task exists in DB but not in PRD JSON → warning logged, DB transition stands, JSON unchanged

Known-bad: a `find("<task-status>")` approach that greedy-matches through the CLOSING `</task-status>` of a later tag would corrupt all subsequent parses. Test: `<task-status>A:done</task-status> noise <task-status>B:done</task-status>` must yield TWO updates, not one with an oversized task_id.
Failure mode: if the existing `complete()` function returns an error (e.g., state machine violation), log the error with `eprintln!` including task_id and status, and proceed to the next tag
userStories.description:
Add a new side-band tag parser — parallel to `extract_key_decisions` and `extract_reorder_task_id` in `src/loop_engine/detection.rs` — that scans Claude's stdout for one or more `<task-status>TASK-ID:status</task-status>` tags and returns `Vec<TaskStatusUpdate>`. Hook it into `src/loop_engine/engine.rs` after `analyze_output` so each parsed update is dispatched through the EXISTING status command (`complete` / `fail` / `skip` / `irrelevant` / `unblock` or `reset_tasks`), THEN also flips the corresponding entry's `passes` field in the PRD JSON on disk (symmetric with `task-mgr add` — every task-mgr operation updates DB + JSON atomically, or neither).

Why side-band not outcome: like key-decisions, status updates don't change the iteration's IterationOutcome classification — they're metadata applied alongside whatever outcome detection returned.

DO:
- Add `pub struct TaskStatusUpdate { pub task_id: String, pub status: TaskStatusChange }` and `pub enum TaskStatusChange { Done, Failed, Skipped, Irrelevant, Blocked }` to detection.rs (or a new module if cleaner)
- Add `pub fn extract_status_updates(output: &str) -> Vec<TaskStatusUpdate>`
- In engine.rs, after the existing PromptTooLong handler block, iterate parsed updates and dispatch
- For `Done`: the existing `complete()` in src/commands/complete.rs enforces the task state machine (learning [1475]: tasks must be `in_progress` before `complete`). If the tag fires for a task not yet claimed, `complete()` will error — catch this, log a clear warning, and continue. Do NOT silently auto-claim — that would mask bugs where Claude marks an unclaimed task done.
- Status parsing must be case-insensitive (`done`, `DONE`, `Done` all valid); task ID parsing is case-sensitive (IDs are case-sensitive in the DB)
- AFTER a successful DB status transition, ALSO update the PRD JSON on disk. Create a helper `fn sync_task_status_to_prd_json(conn, task_id, new_passes: bool, new_status: &str) -> TaskMgrResult<()>`. Reuse the `locate_prd_json` pattern from src/commands/add.rs (query prd_files for file_type='task_list'). Read the JSON, find the matching `userStories[i]` by id, set `passes = true` for Done, leave `passes = false` for the other statuses but record the status somehow (options: add a `status` field to that userStories entry, OR just flip `passes` for Done only and let export/doctor reconcile the rest — recommend the latter to keep the JSON schema tight). Use the same atomic-rename write pattern as add.rs — factor it out if duplication emerges.
- If the PRD JSON sync fails AFTER the DB transition succeeded, log a clear warning (DB-and-JSON drift) but do NOT roll back the DB — the next `task-mgr export` / `doctor` can reconcile. This matches the existing failure mode in add.rs.

DO NOT:
- Introduce new SQL for status transitions — dispatch through existing command handlers
- Add `<task-status>` to the `IterationOutcome` enum — it's side-band
- Use `output.find("<task-status>")` alone — a malformed tag with no close tag must not block parsing of a well-formed tag later in the output (learning [193] pattern: substring match is a common footgun)
- Duplicate the atomic-write code from add.rs — factor it into a shared helper in a new module (`src/commands/prd_json.rs`) OR export the existing `atomic_write` from add.rs as `pub(crate)`.

Edge cases (MUST handle, each gets an explicit test):
- Multiple tags in one output → all applied in order; PRD JSON flipped ONCE per final status after all tags applied
- Malformed tag (no close) → skipped; subsequent well-formed tags still parsed
- Unknown status string (e.g., `:completed`) → logged and skipped; does not crash
- Empty task ID (`<task-status>:done</task-status>`) → skipped; logged
- Task ID with whitespace → trimmed before dispatch
- Task not in DB → complete/fail/skip/irrelevant returns an error; log the warning, keep processing remaining tags, do NOT touch PRD JSON
- Task not in PRD JSON (only in DB — e.g., added via raw SQL) → log warning, DB is still authoritative
- PRD JSON file deleted after iteration started → log warning, DB is still authoritative
- Two tags for the same task_id in one output (e.g., `:done` then `:failed`) → apply in order; last write wins for the JSON flip
edgeCases: Multiple `<task-status>` tags in one output
Malformed tag with missing close (earlier in output) followed by well-formed tag (later)
Unknown status string like `:in_progress` or `:completed` — skipped with log
Empty task_id (`<task-status>:done</task-status>`) — skipped
Whitespace in/around task id — trimmed
Tag emitted for a task not in DB — dispatch error logged, loop continues
Tag emitted for a task not claimed (violates state machine per learning [1475]) — complete() errors, log warning, do not auto-claim
Output with NO tags — extract_status_updates returns empty Vec, no-op dispatcher

failureModes.expectedBehavior: Skip the malformed tag; log once; continue parsing remaining tags

failureModes.cause: complete() / fail() / etc. returns error (task not found, state machine violation)
failureModes.expectedBehavior: Log eprintln! with task_id + status + error; proceed to the next update

userStories.notes:
Files to read: src/loop_engine/detection.rs (existing tag parsers — especially `extract_reorder_task_id` and `extract_key_decisions`; copy their slicing style), src/loop_engine/engine.rs (around line 627 for the analyze_output call + PromptTooLong handler — your dispatch goes AFTER that block, BEFORE update_trackers), src/commands/complete.rs (existing `complete(conn, task_ids, run_id, commit, force)` signature), src/commands/fail.rs, src/commands/skip.rs, src/commands/irrelevant.rs, src/commands/add.rs (for the `locate_prd_json` + `append_task_to_prd_json` + `atomic_write` pattern you'll reuse for the JSON sync).

PRD JSON sync for status tags: the user explicitly chose symmetry — every task-mgr operation updates DB + JSON atomically or neither. For Done, flip `passes=true` in the matching userStories entry. For Failed/Skipped/Irrelevant/Blocked, decide in implementation: either add a `status` field to the userStories entry OR leave those non-Done flips to `task-mgr export` / `doctor` reconciliation (recommend the latter to keep the JSON schema tight — only Done flips `passes`).

Factor out the atomic-write helper: add.rs has `fn atomic_write(target: &Path, content: &str)` that should become a shared utility. Options: move to `src/commands/prd_json.rs` (new module), or `pub(crate)` it from add.rs. Decide in implementation, keep it clean.

Learnings:
- [1475]: Task state machine — tasks must be claimed (in_progress) before complete. The dispatcher must NOT auto-claim because that hides state-machine violations.
- [193]: String find() is a common footgun for tag parsing. Use the extract-inner pattern from extract_reorder_task_id (two-stage slice between open+close tags), iterated until no more tags found.
- [130] / [132]: For tests, use deterministic short strings — don't build giant outputs unless testing boundary behaviour.
- [1361]: Wiring failure mode — when adding a new path that updates multiple stores (DB + JSON), every test must verify BOTH halves update. A test that only asserts DB state would let JSON-sync silently break.

correctness: Tag parser handles malformed input without panic — every unwrap must be justified or replaced with `?` / match
Dispatcher must preserve iteration semantics: status updates happen AFTER analyze_output and BEFORE update_trackers so crash counters still reflect the real outcome
Unknown status variants are logged and skipped, never silently mapped to a nearby variant
performance: Single pass over the output for all tags — do NOT call extract_status_updates multiple times
Tag parsing is O(n) in output length
style: Follow the string-slicing idiom from `extract_reorder_task_id` — no regex dependency needed
TaskStatusChange enum derives Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq
TaskStatusUpdate struct derives Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq (for easy test assertions)

userStories.title: `<task-status>` tag parser + engine dispatch + PRD JSON sync

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: `src/loop_engine/prompt_sections/task_ops.rs` exists and exports `task_ops_section()`
Positive: the section is wired into prompt assembly (grep the prompt builder for `task_ops` and confirm it's called)
Positive: every loop iteration's built prompt contains the exact strings `You MUST NOT edit \`tasks/*.json\`` and `task-mgr add --stdin` and `<task-status>TASK-ID:done</task-status>`
Positive: section positioned ABOVE the learnings section in the assembled prompt (test by locating both substrings and asserting ordering)
Negative: section does NOT reference `.task-mgr/tasks/*.json` — the user corrected this path to `tasks/*.json`
Negative: section is NOT added to any trimmable-sections list — verify by grep
Negative: section is NOT behind a runtime flag or config option
Unit test: prompt_sections::task_ops::tests::test_section_contains_critical_phrases — checks for `MUST NOT edit`, `task-mgr add --stdin`, `<task-status>`, all 5 status keywords
Integration test (in prompt builder test file): test_iteration_prompt_includes_task_ops_section — builds a prompt for a dummy task, asserts the section is present and positioned correctly

Known-bad: a section that uses ``.task-mgr/tasks/*.json`` matches the codebase's internal path but NOT what Claude sees in the loop working directory. Claude's CWD is the project root where the PRD path is `tasks/*.json` — test must assert the literal `tasks/*.json` string, not the longer path
Failure mode: if the section is too long (>500 bytes), prompt budget may be exceeded — verify section.len() < 1024
userStories.description:
Create `src/loop_engine/prompt_sections/task_ops.rs` exporting a static markdown section that gets injected into every loop iteration's prompt. This is hard rules, not hints — sits near the top, above learnings/synergy.

Section text (EXACTLY this — the user corrected the path string from `.task-mgr/tasks/*.json` to `tasks/*.json`):

```markdown
## Task lifecycle — CLI only, never edit the JSON

You MUST NOT edit `tasks/*.json` directly. Instead:

- **Mark a task's status**: emit `<task-status>TASK-ID:done</task-status>`
  (statuses: `done`, `failed`, `skipped`, `irrelevant`, `blocked`). The loop
  engine parses these and applies them via `task-mgr`.
- **Add a new task** (review fix / refactor / follow-up): pipe a single task
  JSON to `task-mgr add --stdin`. Example:

      echo '{"id":"CODE-FIX-001","title":"Fix race in X","difficulty":"medium","touchesFiles":["src/foo.rs"],"dependsOn":[]}' \
        | task-mgr add --stdin

  Priority is auto-computed to rank ahead of the current `next` pick. Omit it
  unless you explicitly need a lower priority.
- For anything else (dependencies, status queries, etc.), use the `task-mgr`
  CLI — see `task-mgr --help`.
```

DO:
- Create the new file with a `pub fn task_ops_section() -> &'static str` returning the text
- Register in `src/loop_engine/prompt_sections/mod.rs` alongside the existing sections (escalation, learnings, etc.)

- Position: near the top of the prompt, above the learnings and synergy sections (this is hard rules — agent must see it before context-sensitive content)
- Section budget: <200 tokens. Do not pad.

DO NOT:
- Add it to the trimmable-sections list — this is non-negotiable content
- Reference `.task-mgr/tasks/*.json` — the user explicitly corrected this to `tasks/*.json`
- Create a config option to disable it

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: researched and documented whether Claude CLI --allowedTools supports negative patterns (decision written in PR description or a commit note)
Positive: loop's allowed-tools policy prevents Edit/Write on any path matching `tasks/*.json` OR loop's post-iteration output includes a targeted hint when the agent attempts one
Positive: `Bash(task-mgr add:*)` and `Bash(task-mgr *)` remain allowed
Positive: `Read` on `tasks/*.json` remains allowed
Negative: No modifications to user's ~/.claude/settings.json
Negative: No blanket `Edit(!*)` — only scoped to `tasks/*.json`
Unit test: config::tests::test_coding_allowed_tools_denies_edit_on_tasks_json (if going the allowed-tools route) — grep the built tools string for the deny pattern
Unit test: config::tests::test_coding_allowed_tools_permits_task_mgr_bash — grep for `Bash(task-mgr:*)` or equivalent
End-to-end test (manual, documented in the commit): run one loop iteration and attempt Edit on tasks/foo.json; verify it's denied and the hint mentions task-mgr add / <task-status>
Known-bad: a policy that denies ALL Edit operations would block legitimate src/**.rs edits. Test MUST verify `Edit` is still allowed for paths NOT matching `tasks/*.json`
Failure mode: if Claude CLI doesn't support negative patterns at all, document this in the commit, fall back to post-iteration detection + hint
userStories.description:
Tighten the loop's allowed-tools policy so `Edit` and `Write` on `tasks/*.json` are denied. When the agent accidentally tries to edit the PRD JSON, it should surface as a denied-tool hint — the agent can then retry via `task-mgr add --stdin` or `<task-status>` tag.

STEP 1 — Research: Read `src/loop_engine/claude.rs` for how the `--allowedTools` string is built for subprocess invocation. Determine empirically whether Claude Code CLI supports NEGATIVE patterns like `Edit(!tasks/*.json)` or requires a separate deny list. Try it locally with `claude --allowedTools 'Edit(!tasks/*.json)' --print '...'` and verify.

STEP 2 — Implement:
- If negative patterns ARE supported: add them to the CODING_ALLOWED_TOOLS string in src/loop_engine/config.rs, scoped to Edit and Write only (NOT Bash — we want `Bash(task-mgr *)` allowed)
- If negative patterns are NOT supported: add post-iteration detection. Parse `permission_denials` from `claude_result` (already captured in engine.rs at line ~527 — see `extract_denied_commands`). If the denial is on `Edit`/`Write` against a `tasks/*.json` path, emit a tailored hint: `[hint] Tool denied: Edit on tasks/*.json — use 'task-mgr add --stdin' or <task-status> tag instead`

DO NOT:
- Block `Bash(task-mgr *)` — this is the intended replacement
- Block `Read` on `tasks/*.json` — agents legitimately read the PRD for context
- Touch the user's global `~/.claude/settings.json` — changes must be loop-local

Edge cases:
- Agent reads `tasks/foo.json` (allowed) then tries to edit it (denied) — hint should say `use 'task-mgr add --stdin' OR <task-status>`
- Agent tries `Write` (create new file) at `tasks/new-thing.json` — also denied; agent must use `task-mgr init --from-json --append` for new PRDs
edgeCases: Agent tries Write (new file creation) at `tasks/new.json` → denied, hint mentions `task-mgr init --append`
Agent tries Edit at `tasks/subdir/foo.json` → matches `tasks/*.json`? Pattern must be explicit about shallow vs recursive (pick one, document)
Agent uses `cd tasks && edit foo.json` — this is Bash, not Edit; the guard doesn't apply. Document as out-of-scope.

failureModes.cause: Claude CLI silently ignores a malformed negative pattern
failureModes.expectedBehavior: Test must verify the guard actually triggers — don't trust that `--allowedTools` consumed the flag means it honored it

userStories.notes:
Files to read: src/loop_engine/config.rs (CODING_ALLOWED_TOOLS constant), src/loop_engine/claude.rs (how --allowedTools is assembled + how `extract_denied_commands` works), src/loop_engine/engine.rs:527 (existing permission-denial hint code — extend this for the fallback path).

Important: verify negative-pattern support empirically before committing to a pattern. If the CLI silently ignores unknown pattern syntax, the guard becomes a no-op and nothing tests catch it.

If fallback needed: the detection path already exists in engine.rs:527 — you're just adding a more specific hint for Edit/Write on tasks/*.json paths.

correctness: Denies Edit AND Write (creating a new file there is also blocked), scoped to `tasks/*.json`
Does NOT deny Read — agents legitimately read the PRD for task selection
Does NOT deny Bash(task-mgr:*) — this is the intended replacement
Works regardless of whether the agent uses relative or absolute paths (pattern matching must cover both)
performance: No per-tool-call overhead beyond what the existing permission flow already does
style: If using allowed-tools route: add to the existing CODING_ALLOWED_TOOLS string, don't create a parallel config
If using fallback-detection route: extend the existing denial-hint code, don't duplicate

userStories.title: Permission guard — deny Edit/Write on `tasks/*.json` via loop's --allowedTools policy

acceptanceCriteria: Positive: `task-mgr add --stdin --depended-on-by MILESTONE-1` (with MILESTONE-1 existing in DB) inserts the new task AND a task_relationships row where task_id=MILESTONE-1, related_id=<new-id>, rel_type='dependsOn'
Positive: PRD JSON's MILESTONE-1 entry gains `<new-id>` in its dependsOn array after the call
Positive: flag accepts multiple targets: `--depended-on-by A --depended-on-by B` — BOTH existing tasks now depend on the new one
Positive: MILESTONE-1 in `task-mgr show MILESTONE-1` now lists the new task in its dependsOn
Negative: `--depended-on-by NONEXISTENT-ID` fails BEFORE any write — no new task in DB, no PRD JSON edit
Negative: the new task's OWN `dependsOn` is NOT populated from --depended-on-by (direction is reversed)
Negative: no new SQL tables — reuses task_relationships
Unit test: `commands::add::tests::test_depended_on_by_inserts_reverse_relationship` — seed MILESTONE-1, call add_with_conn with depended_on_by=['MILESTONE-1'], assert task_relationships has the row
Unit test: `commands::add::tests::test_depended_on_by_invalid_id_rejects_before_insert` — assert new task NOT in DB after the error
Unit test: `commands::add::tests::test_depended_on_by_multiple_targets_all_wired` — seed 2 targets, pass both, assert both relationships inserted
Integration test: `tests/add_integration.rs::test_depended_on_by_updates_prd_json_dependson` — real PRD file, assert the existing task's dependsOn array gains the new id
Integration test: `tests/add_integration.rs::test_depended_on_by_missing_target_in_prd_json_logs_but_dbstill_updated`
CLI test: `task-mgr add --help` documents the --depended-on-by flag

Known-bad: an implementation that mutates the new task's dependsOn (instead of the existing task's) would pass a test checking 'dependsOn contains MILESTONE-1' on the new task — test must assert the REVERSE direction (existing task now has new id in its dependsOn)
Failure mode: if PRD JSON sync fails for one of several targets, DB rows all commit, warning logged per missing target, overall command returns OK (DB-authoritative)
userStories.description:
The existing review workflows (CODE-REVIEW, REFACTOR-REVIEW, MILESTONE gates) create FIX/REFACTOR tasks dynamically and need to add them to the MILESTONE's `dependsOn` so the gate waits. Without CLI support, the loop agent would have to edit `tasks/*.json` — exactly what we're avoiding.

Add a `--depended-on-by <existing-task-id>` flag to `task-mgr add` that, after the new task is inserted, ALSO inserts a `task_relationships` row `(task_id=<existing>, rel_type='dependsOn', related_id=<new>)` — meaning the existing task now depends on the new one — AND updates the existing task's userStories entry in the PRD JSON to include the new task's id in its `dependsOn` array.

DO:
- Add `#[arg(long)] depended_on_by: Vec<String>` to the `Commands::Add` variant (Vec so multiple existing tasks can depend on the new one in one call)
- Thread it through `commands::add::add()` → `add_with_conn()` — extend the signature with `depended_on_by: &[String]`
- Validate every `depended_on_by` id exists in the DB BEFORE inserting the new task (fail fast if any is invalid)
- Inside the existing transaction (after insert_task_relationships for the new task's OWN dependsOn): for each `depended_on_by_id`, call `insert_relationship(&tx, depended_on_by_id, &new_task.id, "dependsOn")` — note the argument order: `(task_id=<existing>, related_id=<new>, rel_type=dependsOn)`
- PRD JSON sync: in addition to appending the new task to userStories, for each `depended_on_by_id`, find its entry in userStories, push `new_task.id` into its `dependsOn` array (create the array if missing)
- If PRD JSON sync partially succeeds (e.g., new task appended but one of the existing entries not found), the DB write has already committed — log a clear warning per missing entry; DB is authoritative

DO NOT:
- Mutate the new task's OWN dependsOn based on depended_on_by — the direction is reversed (the EXISTING task depends on the NEW task)
- Silently skip invalid `depended_on_by` ids — fail the entire operation before any insert
- Introduce new tables or columns — reuse `task_relationships` (already has rel_type='dependsOn') and `userStories[].dependsOn`

Edge cases:
- `--depended-on-by` passed with no new-task JSON via stdin/--json → clap's normal validation covers this
- `--depended-on-by MILESTONE-1,MILESTONE-2` (comma-separated) vs repeated flag (`--depended-on-by MILESTONE-1 --depended-on-by MILESTONE-2`) → prefer repeated flag (clap's default for Vec<String> with `long`)
- `--depended-on-by MISSING-TASK-ID` → fail with a clear error listing the missing id; no DB changes; new task NOT created
- `--depended-on-by` references a task that's in DB but NOT in the PRD JSON (e.g., added via raw SQL) → DB insert succeeds, PRD JSON sync logs a warning for that specific entry, continues
- Attempting to create a dependency cycle (`task A --depended-on-by B`, where B already depends on A transitively) → out of scope for this task; document in commit that cycle detection is a future enhancement
- Duplicate insertion (calling add twice with same `--depended-on-by`) → task_relationships may or may not have a UNIQUE constraint on (task_id, related_id, rel_type); if it does, return a clear error; if not, the second row is harmless noise
edgeCases: --depended-on-by with missing target → fail before any write
--depended-on-by with multiple targets → all inserted, all PRD JSON entries updated
Target exists in DB but not PRD JSON → DB write OK, PRD JSON logs warning for that one target
Empty --depended-on-by (flag not passed) → behaviour identical to current `task-mgr add`
PRD JSON's target entry has NO dependsOn key → create it as a new array with the one id
Dependency cycle creation → OUT OF SCOPE; document in commit for a future task

failureModes.cause: One of the --depended-on-by ids doesn't exist in the DB
failureModes.expectedBehavior: Fail with an error naming the missing id; no writes occur

failureModes.cause: PRD JSON write fails after DB commit
failureModes.expectedBehavior: Log warning; DB is authoritative; user can rerun or run task-mgr export to reconcile

userStories.notes:
Files to touch: src/cli/commands.rs (add the flag to Commands::Add), src/main.rs (pass it through), src/commands/add.rs (thread through public API + add reverse-insert logic + PRD-JSON-mutation helper).

For the PRD JSON sync side: reuse the atomic-write helper factored out in FEAT-003 (if completed before this task, which depending on ordering may not be the case — if FEAT-003 hasn't factored it yet, this task can either factor it first or duplicate for now and let REFACTOR-001 deduplicate).

Reverse-insert SQL pattern: `insert_relationship(&tx, depended_on_by_id /* task_id */, &new_task.id /* related_id */, "dependsOn")` — argument 1 is the EXISTING task that now HAS a new dependency; argument 2 is the new task being depended upon.

Learning [1475]: task_relationships may have a UNIQUE(task_id, related_id, rel_type) index — check src/db/schema for this; if present, duplicate calls surface as a clean DB error.

correctness: Direction of dependency: EXISTING task's dependsOn gains the NEW task's id (not the other way around)
Validation happens before any writes — invalid ids cause a clean rejection
All writes (new task + reverse relationships + PRD JSON) share a transaction where possible
PRD JSON partial failure doesn't roll back the DB (DB is authoritative)
performance: Validation is a single IN-clause query for all depended_on_by ids, not N queries
style: Reuse the existing insert_relationship helper in src/commands/init/import.rs:356 — do not inline SQL
Error messages name the specific invalid id, not 'one of the --depended-on-by ids is invalid'

Positive: cargo clippy --lib -- -D warnings clean

userStories.description:
Review all delivered code for quality, security, integration wiring, and documentation needs. Run the full test suite. Perform the end-to-end smoke test from the plan.

DO:
- Run: `cargo test --lib 2>&1 | tee /tmp/t.txt | tail -5 && grep FAILED /tmp/t.txt | head -10` (single command, sandbox-safe — learning [1430])
- Run: `cargo test --test '*' 2>&1 | tee /tmp/it.txt | tail -5 && grep FAILED /tmp/it.txt | head -10`
- Run: `cargo clippy --lib -- -D warnings 2>&1 | tee /tmp/c.txt | tail -5 && grep '^error' /tmp/c.txt | head -10`
- Run: `cargo fmt --check 2>&1 | tee /tmp/f.txt | tail -5 && grep 'Diff' /tmp/f.txt | head -5`
- Verify all new code reachable from production entry points (grep for public fn invocations)
- Check documentation: CLAUDE.md updated with a quick-reference for `task-mgr add` + `<task-status>` tag? Architecture docs updated if new modules introduced? If so, update them.
- Manual smoke test (document results in REVIEW-001's commit message):
  1. `task-mgr init` a test PRD
  2. Note `task-mgr next` pick's priority
  3. Run `echo '{"id":"<prefix>-SMOKE-001","title":"smoke","difficulty":"medium","touchesFiles":[]}' | task-mgr add --stdin`
  4. Assert `task-mgr next` now returns SMOKE-001
  5. Assert PRD JSON on disk has SMOKE-001 as last userStories entry
  6. Produce a test output containing `<task-status>SMOKE-001:done</task-status>`, run through detection — assert extract_status_updates returns the update
- If issues: add FIX-xxx tasks via `task-mgr add --stdin`

DO NOT: Skip the integration-wiring check. Unconnected code is the #1 failure mode (per prompt template). Specifically verify the prompt_sections wiring (FEAT-004) — it's easy to create the module and forget to call it.
userStories.notes:
Use the single-command test pattern with tee/tail/grep — learning [1430]: split commands hit the sandbox approval flow and fail.

The smoke test is best done in a scratch tempdir so you don't pollute the real `.task-mgr/`. Use `mktemp -d` and `cd` into it.

If CLAUDE.md doesn't already have a 'Loop CLI Cheat Sheet' section, add one. Anyone running `task-mgr loop` manually benefits from knowing the tag and add-cli syntax.
userStories.title: Final review: security, integration wiring, documentation, end-to-end smoke
